title: Crack healing induced electrical and mechanical properties recovery in a Ti2SnC ceramic
abstract: Ceramics are able to heal cracks and flaws by triggering local sintering (internal cracks) or oxidation reaction (surface cracks) which may induce the recovery of mechanical strength. Crack healing in ceramics, however, is limited to micro-sized cracks and requires elevated temperatures (above 1000 ◦C) and long time periods even up to 100 h. In this work we report on accelerated crack healing of a Ti2SnC ceramic which is capable to repair thermal shock induced cracks at a relatively low temperature of 800 ◦C within only 1 h. After healing treatment both the low flexural strength and electrical conductivity measured on the damaged material after quenching were almost fully recovered. Furthermore, Ti2SnC exhibits repeatable healing capability which offers a high potential for developing durable ceramics with prolonged lifetime under harsh thermal conditions.
results: The Ti2SnC hot-pressed at 1250 ◦C is characterized by an average particle size of 1.5 m and a relative density of 98%. Ti2SnC is the dominating phase with only minor fractions of Sn and Ti6Sn5 detected by XRD. Thermally activated decomposition of Ti2SnC is likely to yield intermetallic Ti–Sn phases as well as elemental Sn during sintering or hot-pressing at elevated temperatures. Annealing of bulk Ti2SnC samples in air at 600 ◦C revealed SnO2 with a little amount of TiO2 as the major surface oxidation products. One cycle of quenching at different temperatures results in a decrease in the residual electrical conductivity. The electrical conductivity after thermal shock at 700 ◦C decreased by 7% as compared to the initial value due to the formation of multiple cracks on the quenched surfaces. After one cycle of healing at 800 ◦C, the recovered strengths are greatly improved, close to the initial strength or even higher.
discussion: Penetration of oxygen from the environmental atmosphere into the crack causes Ti2SnC at the crack surface to undergo an oxidation reaction which was reported to follow two stages. At temperatures below 800 °C destabilization of the MAX phase crystal lattice tends to release Sn leaving a distorted Ti2Sn_{1-x}C phase. At higher temperatures TiO2 formation tends to be dominative due to the lower standard Gibbs free energy of TiO2 formation compared to that of SnO2. If the oxygen potential is high enough, SnO2 may be formed as observed in large cracks at least near to the surface. The high mobility of Sn in Ti2SnC driven by its low migration energy as well as fluid flow transport above its low melting point seems to favor transport of Sn even into small cracks and micro cracks with narrow crack opening. The presence of metallic Sn in the healed cracks should play a key role in recovery of the electrical conductivity.
conclusion: Healing of thermal shock induced crack patterns in the Ti2SnC ceramic was investigated. During annealing in air atmosphere oxygen may penetrate into cracks and react with Ti2SnC on the crack surface. In large cracks near the surface a high oxygen potential triggers the formation of TiO2 and SnO2 filling the crack space. Limitation of oxygen transport in small secondary cracks gives rise to a low oxygen potential and metallic Sn remains. Due to restoration of solid contact in the crack, flexural strength and electrical conductivity recovered almost to the values of the virgin material after healing at 800 °C for 1 h. The capability of healing even large cracks and crack patterns at moderate temperature and within a short time period offers a high potential for improving the performance and lifetime of the Ti2SnC ceramic applied at elevated temperatures in air atmosphere.
